content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Distinguish source tokens, parse5-repaired tree, Cheerio selections, browser DOM, accessibility tree, and rendered state.

hints: For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend the Source Tree Distinction model · Theory Source Tr…, name the owner, authorization, source and emitted revision, Node, TypeScript, URL, robots, unknown value, Fetch, Promise, AbortSignal, browser, accessibility, report, handle, recovery, and native-transfer boundary, Source Tree Distinction precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend the Source Tree Distinction model · Theory Source Tr…, trace this concrete input and predict values, errors, blocking, and output before reviewing it: A theory TypeScript team handles case 8360 during an origin-fairness and event-loop capacity rehearsal. Build deterministic TypeScript evidence to prove Cheerio loading modes, malformed tree repair, parser differentials, budgets, and source-tree distinctions; every external value remains unknown until runtime admission produces an owned value; then change one HTML repair, Cheerio mode, selector, unknown value, record schema, link relation, or metadata case and retain rejected evidence. Browser TypeScript uses pure bounded models and original URL, robots, sitemap, HTTP, HTML, frontier, accessibility, browser, and report fixtures only. Real DNS, Fetch, streams, Cheerio, Playwright, axe-core, files, processes, packages, containers, assistive-technology review, load, legal review, restore, and production behavior require explicit controlled transfer gates. Competency probe: Distinguish source tokens, parse5-repaired tree, Cheerio selections, browser DOM, accessibility tree, and rendered state.

For Authorized site auditing and TypeScript Defend the Source Tree Distinction model · Theory Source Tr…, challenge the documented misconception with runtime, failure, race, or toolchain evidence: HTML source and browser DOM always have identical structure.
